Principle: Peripheral vision is used more than central vision
Main Characteristics: Peripheral vision identifies scene type and detects movement/danger faster; central vision is for detail. Users rely on it for initial assessment and visual anchors.
Do's: Ensure the periphery communicates site purpose; use high-quality thumbnails; place elements for 'glanceable' info in the periphery; use peripheral cues to draw attention to changes.
Don'ts: Don't place vital info where it can be overlooked due to banner blindness; don't use heavy peripheral animation that distracts from the primary task; don't assume users only see the center focus.
Other Characteristics: Psychological Category: How People See; Platforms: Desktop, Mobile, Web, Digital Interfaces.
Severity: High - Fundamental biological constraint for survival and situational awareness.

Principle: The Limbic System drives decision making
Main Characteristics: Decisions are controlled by the part of the brain that does not control language; it is based on 'feeling' and the 'Why'.
Do's: Communicate the purpose/benefit ('Why') before the features ('What').
Don'ts: Don't rely solely on facts, figures, and technical features to persuade users.

Principle: The human body is built on the proportions of the Golden Section.
Main Characteristics: Ancient and modern architectural canons are linked to human measures (ell, thumb, foot).
Do's: Use scale and proportion derived from human body ratios (Modulor) to design spaces.
Don'ts: Ignore human eye level (avg. 170 cm) when placing visual information.
Other Characteristics: How People See; Human Biological Constraints
Severity: High - Fundamental biological basis for harmony.

Principle: Medium type width is the easiest type to read.
Main Characteristics: Optimal balance between character width and spacing (condensed vs. expanded).
Do's: Choose medium-width typefaces for primary text body.
Don'ts: Use extra-condensed or extra-expanded type for long reading passages.
Severity: High - Fundamental biological constraint of the eye.

Principle: Single Responsibility Principle (SOLID)
Main Characteristics: A class should have just one reason to change, meaning it should be responsible for a single part of the functionality.
Do's: Make every class responsible for a single part of functionality; encapsulate that responsibility entirely.
Don'ts: Allow a class to perform multiple unrelated tasks (e.g., managing data and printing reports).
Severity: Critical - Prevents brain stack overflow and losing control over growing codebases.

Principle: Liskov Substitution Principle (SOLID)
Main Characteristics: Subclasses should be passable in place of parent classes without breaking the client code.
Do's: Keep parameter types match or be more abstract than superclass; keep return types match or be more specific; preserve invariants.
Don'ts: Strengthen pre-conditions in subclasses; weaken post-conditions; throw unexpected exceptions not defined by the base method.
Severity: Critical - Fundamental constraint for library/framework development and polymorphism.

Principle: Principle 1: Satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software
Main Characteristics: Focuses on frequent updates and versioning to meet changing customer needs and keep up with competition.
Do's: Prioritize features based on market demand and include the UX team early in the process.
Don'ts: Haphazardly put together features or ignore design quality in the rush to deliver.
Other Characteristics: How People See (UX lens); All platforms including apps and websites.

Principle: Principle 7: Working software is the primary measure of progress
Main Characteristics: Prioritizes the creation of actual product over graphs, metrics, or supporting artifacts.
Do's: Judge UX progress based on designs successfully implemented in the shipping software.
Don'ts: Focus exclusively on artifacts like specifications that may never be built.
Other Characteristics: How People See (UX lens); Production environment.

Principle: Principle 9: Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design
Main Characteristics: Agility is enhanced by refining technology and user experience throughout the cycle.
Do's: Treat design as a living thing that is constantly revised based on feedback.
Don'ts: Treat a specification as a finished document that cannot be revisited.
Other Characteristics: How People See (UX lens); Design paradigm context.

Principle: Principle 12: Regular reflection on effectiveness
Main Characteristics: The team tunes and adjusts its behavior at regular intervals to become more effective.
Do's: Hold productive retrospective meetings where feedback is heard and acted upon.
Don'ts: Shut down conversation or ignore team feedback during reflection sessions.
Other Characteristics: How People Decide (UX lens); Team improvement context.

Principle: Not all elements are equal
Main Characteristics: Visual hierarchy is the most effective tool for making a design look 'designed'.
Do's: De-emphasize secondary and tertiary information to highlight what matters.
Don'ts: Don't let every element compete for attention, creating a 'wall of content'.

Principle: People see cues that tell them what to do with an object
Main Characteristics: Affordances are perceived action possibilities (e.g., a button's shadow suggests pushing).
Do's: Use shading and shadows to show when an object is clickable or active.
Don'ts: Use hover cues for touch-only devices like the iPad.
Other Characteristics: How People See; UI Design/Physical objects.

Principle: A Button Should Look Like a Button
Main Characteristics: Interactive elements must be visually distinct and follow familiar forms to be recognized as actionable.
Do's: Use rectangles or rounded rectangles; ensure the button stands out from the background.
Don'ts: Use organic or irregular shapes that dissolve the function; remove too many visual cues so it looks like plain text.
Other Characteristics: UI Design; How People See; Interaction Design.
Severity: Critical - Biological/Cognitive constraint for quick identification and usage.

Principle: There's a special part of the brain just for recognizing faces
Main Characteristics: The fusiform face area (FFA) processes faces faster than objects and is linked to the emotional center.
Do's: Use up-close faces looking at the camera for emotional impact; use faces looking at products to direct attention.
Don'ts: Assume autistic users process faces using the FFA.

Principle: Proper Product Data is Vital to E-Commerce Usability
Main Characteristics: Lack of structured or consistent data severely impedes product finding and comparison.
Do's: Develop automated and manual verification processes; harmonize supplier specs; ensure naming and unit consistency.
Don'ts: Don't use unstructured blobs of text; don't direct-import vendor spec sheets without post-processing.

Principle: Allow Users to Combine Multiple Filter Values of the Same Filter Type
Main Characteristics: Mutually exclusive filters force users to repeat the filtering flow multiple times.
Do's: Allow 'OR' logic for values within a type; use checkboxes to indicate combinability.
Don'ts: Don't refresh the page and clear old selections when a new value is clicked; don't make color/size options exclusive.

Principle: Feature a sufficiently broad range of product types on your homepage
Main Characteristics: First-time users rely on homepage content and main navigation to determine site type and catalog breadth. Narrow selections lead to misinterpretation.
Do's: Ensure a broad selection of different product types are featured; dedicate space to various categories even if some are more popular; use images or thumbnails for featured product types.
Don'ts: Don't feature only a small selection of product types; don't allow users to conclude the site has a narrow range because only one type (e.g. shoes) is visible.
Other Characteristics: Homepage; E-commerce platform; How People See
Severity: Harmful (Harmful to brand perception and sales)

Principle: Filters vs. categories
Main Characteristics: Categories are mutually exclusive (part of hierarchy); filters narrow a list by attributes (not mutually exclusive). Mixing them up limits search capability.
Do's: Implement product type, brand, and style as filters when attributes are shared; use categories only to serve as scopes that share distinct attributes (like 'size').
Don'ts: Don't implement product style as a category if it prevents combining criteria (e.g. 'down' + 'mummy' sleeping bags).
Other Characteristics: Category Taxonomy; Logic/Mental Models; How People Find
Severity: Harmful (Critical barrier to finding specific items)

Principle: Equitable Use
Main Characteristics: The design is useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities.
Do's: Provide identical means of use for all users whenever possible; make the design appealing to all.
Don'ts: Segregate or stigmatize any users; provide inconsistent privacy or safety measures based on ability.
Severity: High - Fundamental human rights and accessibility constraint.

Principle: Tolerance for Error
Main Characteristics: The design minimizes hazards and adverse consequences of accidental actions.
Do's: Arrange elements to minimize hazards; provide fail-safe features; discourage unconscious action in hazardous tasks.
Don'ts: Place critical 'delete' or 'stop' buttons where they can be hit accidentally without confirmation.

Principle: Perceptible Information
Main Characteristics: The design communicates necessary information effectively regardless of ambient conditions or sensory abilities.
Do's: Use redundant pictorial, verbal, or tactile cues; provide adequate contrast.
Don'ts: Rely on a single sensory channel (e.g., sound only) for critical alerts.
Severity: High - Essential for safety and inclusive interaction.

Principle: Normal spacing between letters is essential for legibility in photo-typesetting.
Main Characteristics: Too narrow a setting of letters makes reading difficult.
Do's: Insist on normal spacing (spationierung) when ordering photo-typesetting.
Don'ts: Use narrow or irregular letter spacing that impairs word recognition.
Severity: High (Legibility is a critical failure point)

Principle: The right width of column is essential for an even reading rhythm.
Main Characteristics: Optimal line length prevents fatigue; usually 7 to 10 words per line for normal reading distance.
Do's: Calculate column width based on typeface size and a 30-35 cm reading distance.
Don'ts: Use overlong lines (strenuous to track) or overshort lines (energy wasted on frequent line changes).

Principle: Good leading carries the eye optically from one line to the next.
Main Characteristics: Vertical distance between lines provides stability and confidence during reading.
Do's: Use sufficient leading to prevent the eye from taking in neighboring lines at the same time.
Don'ts: Set lines too narrowly (impairs speed) or too widely (breaks link between lines).

Principle: Information presented with clear and logically set out hierarchies is better understood and retained.
Main Characteristics: Clear titles, subtitles, texts, illustrations, and captions facilitate faster reading and better memorability.
Do's: Organize text and pictures systematically; use clear and logically set out titles and captions.
Don'ts: Mix different styles of typefaces (e.g., Helvetica with Univers) or use inconsistent positioning.
Severity: High (Direct impact on cognitive load and memory retention)

Principle: Contrast between type sizes must be clearly recognizable.
Main Characteristics: Clear differences in size and weight (e.g., 9-pt vs 6-pt) facilitate categorization of information.
Do's: Use distinct colour values (light grey for medium, deep grey for bold) to differentiate text levels.
Don'ts: Mix characters of the same style/family (e.g., Helvetica with Univers) in a way that creates ambiguity.

Principle: Dependency Inversion Principle (SOLID)
Main Characteristics: High-level classes shouldn't depend on low-level classes; both should depend on abstractions.
Do's: Describe interfaces for low-level operations in business terms; make high-level logic depend on these interfaces.
Don'ts: Make business logic classes dependent on primitive low-level details like disk writing or network protocols.
Severity: High (Enables switching low-level details without affecting core business logic)

Principle: Open/Closed Principle (SOLID)
Main Characteristics: Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification to prevent breaking existing code when adding features.
Do's: Produce subclasses to override parts of a class for different behavior; use the Strategy pattern.
Don'ts: Change the code of a class directly once it is developed and tested (except for bug fixes).
Severity: High (Protects existing clients from regression bugs while allowing growth)

Principle: Favor Composition Over Inheritance
Main Characteristics: Use 'has a' relationships instead of 'is a' relationships to avoid combinatorial explosion of subclasses.
Do's: Delegate behaviors to other objects; use composition/aggregation; replace behavior at runtime.
Don'ts: Extend classes in several independent dimensions (e.g., cargo type x engine type); rely solely on parallel inheritance hierarchies.
Severity: High (Avoids rigid, unmanageable class hierarchies and duplicate code)

Principle: Encapsulate What Varies
Main Characteristics: Identify the aspects of your application that vary and separate them from what stays the same to minimize effects caused by changes.
Do's: Isolate parts of the program that vary in independent modules; divide logic into independent compartments.
Don'ts: Mix tax calculation code or varying logic with the rest of a method's primary code.
Severity: High (Reduces system failure risks and cognitive load during maintenance)

Principle: Principle 2: Welcome changing requirements, even late in development
Main Characteristics: Harnesses change for the customer's competitive advantage rather than viewing it as a disruption.
Do's: Solicit user feedback early and often; use Agile mechanisms to accommodate late revisions.
Don'ts: Defer critical feedback to unspecified future releases that may never happen.
Other Characteristics: How People See (UX lens); General software development.

Principle: Principle 3: Deliver working software frequently
Main Characteristics: Prefers shorter timescales (weeks to months) to allow for frequent influence of design and research.
Do's: Use working code for user research and influenced shipping cycles.
Don'ts: Rely exclusively on rough prototypes or wait until late in the cycle for working code.
Other Characteristics: How People Remember (UX lens); General software development.

Principle: Principle 6: Face-to-face conversation is the most effective method
Main Characteristics: Values in-person interaction to avoid the loss of information from facial expressions and body language.
Do's: Use collaborative whiteboarding and digital sketching tools for remote teams.
Don'ts: Rely solely on email or instant messaging for complex design concepts.
Other Characteristics: How People Communicate (UX lens); Distributed and colocated teams.

Principle: Principle 8: Promote sustainable development
Main Characteristics: Maintains a constant pace that sponsors, developers, and users can sustain indefinitely.
Do's: Develop skills in estimating effort for small increments of work and load balance UX tasks.
Don'ts: Routine 80-hour workweeks or overcommitting the team to unrealistic deadlines.
Other Characteristics: How People Focus (UX lens); Professional workload context.

Principle: Principle 10: Simplicity—the art of maximizing the amount of work not done
Main Characteristics: Focuses on essential tasks and being strategic about which activities are most effective.
Do's: Question the need for wireframes if a design solution is already agreed upon and being coded.
Don'ts: Spend time on extraneous tasks or habits like producing sketches out of a sense of obligation.
Other Characteristics: How People Focus (UX lens); Workflow optimization.

Principle: DesignOps mindset: Focus on the 'how' so design can focus on the 'what'.
Main Characteristics: Operationalizing, optimizing, and scaling design through standardized processes.
Do's: Use common naming structures for files; standardize toolstacks; conduct discovery research on team pain points.
Don'ts: Let talent leave due to boredom or repetitive manual tasks; ignore the human experience of the team.

Principle: Recognition over Recall
Main Characteristics: Users are better at recognizing previously experienced items than recalling them from memory.
Do's: Show recently viewed items; provide clear thumbnails and labels; use familiar icons.
Don'ts: Force users to remember specific paths or item details across screens.
Other Characteristics: Psychological Category: How People Remember; Category: Persuasive UX Patterns.

Principle: One number 1 priority
Main Characteristics: Every screen or section must have a single most important action or message.
Do's: Make the primary CTA visually distinct (e.g., 'Boss' button); hide secondary actions in menus.
Don'ts: Compete for attention with multiple equally styled primary buttons.
Other Characteristics: Psychological Category: How People Focus; Category: Design Principles.

Principle: Time Value / Simplicity
Main Characteristics: Users value efficiency and speed in achieving their goals.
Do's: Make interactions short, simple, and fast; remove needless parts; use clear signposting.
Don'ts: Add friction with unnecessary steps or complex UI decorations that don't serve the task.
Other Characteristics: Psychological Category: Human Motivation; Context: Busy professionals.

Principle: Safe for Exploration (Unbreakable)
Main Characteristics: Design should allow users to explore without fear of making mistakes.
Do's: Make buttons large enough to tap easily; use undo options instead of complex confirmations.
Don'ts: Place destructive actions (Delete) next to frequent positive actions.
Other Characteristics: Psychological Category: How People Feel; Platform: Mobile/Touch.

Principle: The Serial Position Effect
Main Characteristics: The start and end of an experience are the most critical points in determining a person's memory of the event.
Do's: Ensure the first and last interactions are high quality and satisfying ('Start strong, End strong').
Don'ts: Don't neglect the final steps of a user journey, such as confirmation screens or logout.

Principle: SUCCES: The Six Keys of Memorability
Main Characteristics: Ideas stick when they are Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, and use Stories.
Do's: Break complex tasks into simple steps; use concrete language and anecdotes.
Don'ts: Avoid unnecessary abstraction which increases cognitive load.
Other Characteristics: How People Remember; How People Think

Principle: Thumb Zone Ergonomics
Main Characteristics: Most users hold phones one-handed with the thumb anchored at the bottom-right.
Do's: Place primary actions at the bottom of the screen; design for a natural sweeping thumb arc.
Don'ts: Place frequently used buttons in the 'Ow' zone (top-left/top-right); assume users will change their grip to reach controls.
Other Characteristics: How People Move (Ergonomics); Platform: Mobile (Large Screen)

Principle: Transitional Volatility
Main Characteristics: The degree of reorientation a person experiences when moving between pages or touchpoints.
Do's: Design for coherency across all touchpoints; bridge gaps so users don't have to.
Don'ts: Force users to re-learn navigation on every page; provide inconsistent experiences across different channels (web/phone).
Other Characteristics: How People Remember/Navigate; Platform: Multi-channel Systems

Principle: Progressive Disclosure (Implicit)
Main Characteristics: Users are smart but busy; they move from being 'noobs' to intermediates and need relevant info at each stage.
Do's: Focus design efforts on intermediates; provide clear labels rather than abstract icons.
Don'ts: Overwhelm first-time users with 'coach marks' or instructional overlays; treat users as experts from the first interaction.
Other Characteristics: How People Learn; Platform: Software Interfaces

Principle: Visual Weight Priority
Main Characteristics: Contrast, size, and depth direct the user's attention to the most important elements.
Do's: Make important buttons higher contrast; make primary actions larger.
Don'ts: Make the logo the biggest element unless it is the primary goal; use high contrast for secondary/background tasks.
Other Characteristics: How People See; Platform: Visual Design

Principle: Start with a feature, not a layout
Main Characteristics: Design actual functionality instead of the application shell (nav bars, sidebars, logos).
Do's: Start with a piece of actual functionality like 'searching for a flight'.
Don'ts: Don't design the navigation bar or top nav first.

Principle: Don't design too much
Main Characteristics: Avoid designing every single feature and edge case before implementation.
Do's: Work in cycles; build a simple version of the next feature and make it real.
Don'ts: Don't try to figure out every edge case (e.g., 2000 contacts) using only a design tool.

Principle: Limit your choices
Main Characteristics: Reduce decision fatigue by defining restrictive systems in advance.
Do's: Choose from a pre-defined set of 8-10 shades or a specific type scale.
Don'ts: Don't reach for the color picker or tweak font sizes one pixel at a time manually.

Principle: Establish a spacing and sizing system
Main Characteristics: Use a constrained set of values rather than arbitrary pixel counts.
Do's: Use a non-linear scale (e.g., multiples of 16 px) where jumps are at least 25%.
Don'ts: Don't nitpick between 120 px and 125 px.

Principle: Establish a type scale
Main Characteristics: Limit the number of font sizes used in an interface.
Do's: Use a hand-crafted scale (e.g., 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 24, 30...); use rem or px units.
Don'ts: Don't use em units for nested font sizes; avoid mathematical modular scales that create fractions.

Principle: Semantics are secondary
Main Characteristics: Action buttons should be designed based on hierarchy (Primary, Secondary, Tertiary).
Do's: Use solid, high-contrast colors for primary actions; use links for tertiary.
Don'ts: Don't design buttons purely on semantics (e.g., making every 'Delete' button big and red).

Principle: Avoid ambiguous spacing
Main Characteristics: Use spacing to connect related elements and separate distinct groups.
Do's: Ensure there is more space around a group than there is within it.
Don'ts: Don't use the same margin below a label as you do below the input field.

Principle: Users' needs should guide design
Main Characteristics: Primary consideration for all design decisions to help users carry out tasks quickly.
Do's: Structure products so tasks are completed fast. Use skeuomorphic touches for older, less tech-savvy users.
Don'ts: Don't ignore the specific demographics of the target audience.

Principle: Environment influences operation
Main Characteristics: The physical space and platform (TV vs. Mobile) change usage variables.
Do's: Consider viewing distance and input methods (e.g., remote for TV). Adjust text size and contrast for the specific environment.
Don'ts: Don't design for TV the same way you design for a mobile phone.
Other Characteristics: How People See; Platform: TV, Mobile

Principle: Visual Hierarchy guides consumption
Main Characteristics: Layout, color, and typography influence where the eye is drawn.
Do's: Use high contrast and bold type for important items. Place items in the order you want them consumed.
Don'ts: Don't make all elements equal in weight; users won't know where to look first.

Principle: Typography is essential for information
Main Characteristics: Most information is conveyed through text, requiring legible sizes and leading.
Do's: Use at least 12pt font (16pt is better). Keep line lengths between 45-75 characters.
Don'ts: Don't accept default font settings; ensure leading is at least 1.5x type size.

Principle: Color and Shape have specific meanings
Main Characteristics: Norms like green/ticks for 'good' and red/crosses for 'bad' are established.
Do's: Use yellow triangles for warnings and blue circles for information.
Don'ts: Don't jumble these norms as it causes user confusion. Don't rely on color alone for accessibility.

Principle: Clarity prevents cognitive overload
Main Characteristics: Designs should be concise to avoid overwhelming users.
Do's: Show only relevant context; use progressive disclosure for more details.
Don'ts: Don't display all content at once if it isn't necessary for the immediate task.

Principle: People identify objects by recognizing patterns
Main Characteristics: The brain uses 24 basic geometric shapes (geons) to identify objects.
Do's: Use grouping and white space to create patterns; use simple geometric drawings for icons.
Don'ts: Favor 3D elements over 2D, as 3D may slow recognition.

Principle: People scan screens based on past experience and expectations
Main Characteristics: Users look at the center first, avoiding edges, and follow their culture's reading patterns.
Do's: Place important information in the top third or middle of the screen.
Don'ts: Put important content at the edges of the screen.
Other Characteristics: How People See; Computer screens/Web sites.

Principle: People believe that things that are close together belong together
Main Characteristics: Proximity implies relationship, following Gestalt principles.
Do's: Put items in close proximity to show they are related; use whitespace instead of lines to group.
Don'ts: Ignore spacing between unrelated items, which can cause confusion.

Principle: What you see isn't what your brain gets
Main Characteristics: The brain interprets sensory input based on expectations and creates shortcuts like the Kanizsa triangle.
Do's: Influence perception using shapes and colors; consider user background and familiarity.
Don'ts: Assume users see a realistic representation of what is physically there.
Other Characteristics: How People See; universal biological constraint.

Principle: When to Use a List View, a Grid View, or Both
Main Characteristics: Grid views favor visually-driven purchases; list views favor spec-driven purchases with more detail.
Do's: Use Grid View for apparel/furniture; use List View for electronics/tools; ideally switch automatically based on category.
Don'ts: Don't use a 'one size fits all' layout; don't hide vital info (price/title) when switching to grid view on mobile.
Other Characteristics: How People See; Visual vs Spec-driven product verticals.

Principle: Show Total Number of Items in the Currently Displayed Product List
Main Characteristics: Users use the total result count to gauge search quality or category scope appropriateness.
Do's: Display the count above and below the list; integrate count into the title or header.
Don'ts: Don't hide result count; don't make it look like pagination links.

Principle: Use 'Load More' Instead of Endless Scrolling and Pagination
Main Characteristics: Pagination feels slow; endless scrolling makes footers inaccessible. 'Load More' buttons balance flow and control.
Do's: Use 'Load More' with lazy-loading for categories; use 'Load More' alone for search and mobile.
Don'ts: Don't use pagination on mobile; don't use endless scrolling for search results.

Principle: Include Both the User Rating Average and Number of Ratings
Main Characteristics: Users rely on 'Social Proof'; averages are not trusted without seeing the number of votes.
Do's: Always display the count of ratings next to stars; use labels like '11 reviews'.
Don'ts: Don't show a 5-star average alone; don't hide the rating area if there are zero reviews (use 'Not yet rated').

Principle: Always Indicate Essential Product Variations in the List Item
Main Characteristics: Users assume only the displayed version exists if no indicators are present.
Do's: Show color swatches or text labels like '5 more sizes'; show visual previews on hover.
Don'ts: Don't hide variations until click; don't use generic labels like 'More options' when specific ones like '6 screen sizes' fit.

Principle: Numeric Product Specs Need Proper Labels and Units
Main Characteristics: Ambiguous numbers force users to guess meaning, leading to incorrect assessments.
Do's: Include units (e.g., 'Fahrenheit', 'kg'); use explicit labels for all numeric values.
Don'ts: Don't list numbers in isolation; don't assume units are self-evident.

Principle: Logic for Sorting 'Customer Ratings' Should Be Based on User Expectations, Not Arithmetic
Main Characteristics: Users value a 4.5-star product with 50 reviews over a 5-star product with 1 review.
Do's: Use a statistical mix of average and quantity; use a cut-off (e.g., minimum 5 ratings).
Don'ts: Don't sort by raw arithmetic average alone; don't place unvetted 5-star products at the top.
Other Characteristics: How People Think; Trust and Social Proof.

Principle: Avoid placing ads in prime content locations on the homepage
Main Characteristics: Internal ads and banners are perceived negatively, especially when placed in middle columns or top sections. Pop-ups and overlays are viewed as 'spam'.
Do's: Be mindful of size, placement, and integration with overall design; ensure legitimate promotions don't look like external ads.
Don'ts: Avoid pop-up banners or dialog overlays on load; don't use fixed blocks for internal 'ads' that mimic standard ad layouts.
Severity: Disruptive (Causes full stop or negative brand tone)

Principle: Carousel limitations and auto-rotation
Main Characteristics: Users rarely see all slides in a carousel; usually only the first slide. Fast rotation causes frustration; lack of hover-pause leads to misclicks.
Do's: Set rotation time to 5–10 seconds depending on text; pause auto-rotation on mouse hover; permanently stop auto-rotation after any user interaction (clicking next/prev).
Don'ts: Don't make vital site features (like wizards) accessible only via carousel slides; don't rotate slides too quickly.
Other Characteristics: Homepage; Device: Desktop/Laptop (contrast with mobile); How People See
Severity: Disruptive (Users miss content or click wrong links)

Principle: Assist selection of a well-defined scope from the homepage
Main Characteristics: Quickly finding a relevant scope increases success rates. Landing in the wrong scope leads to false conclusions that the site lacks the product.
Do's: List popular categories and sub-categories directly on the homepage; embed wizards or product finders.
Don'ts: Don't force users to plow through the entire taxonomy to reach a defined scope; don't rely only on generic top-level categories.
Other Characteristics: Homepage; Information Architecture; How People Navigate

Principle: Support users' expectation of finding accessories within high-level categories
Main Characteristics: Users expect accessories (e.g. laptop bags) to be nested within the parent product category (e.g. PCs & Laptops) rather than a global 'Accessories' category.
Do's: Place an 'Accessories' sub-category within relevant high-level categories; cross-link related accessory categories.
Don'ts: Don't pollute the default parent category product list with accessory items; keep them in their own sub-category.
Other Characteristics: Category Taxonomy; Mental Models; How People Remember/Group
Severity: Disruptive (Users look in 'logical' place and find nothing)

Principle: Consider having a "drop-down" menu
Main Characteristics: Mega menus allow users to infer catalog depth without clicking. They help avoid detours to wrong parent categories.
Do's: Use a hover delay of 300–500ms; use a mouse-path algorithm to prevent accidental closure when moving diagonally.
Don'ts: Don't make menus 'flickery'; don't show the menu without a delay.
Other Characteristics: Main Navigation; Device: Mouse/Trackpad (Desktop); How People Navigate

Principle: Direct Manipulation
Main Characteristics: Depend on visual representation of objects, physical actions instead of syntax, and immediate visible effects.
Do's: Provide immediate feedback; allow actions to be reversed; align interface actions with user understanding.
Don'ts: Use complex syntax; force users to use logical conditions for simple tasks.
Severity: Medium - Reduces cognitive load and makes interfaces predictable.

Principle: Asynchronous IoT Interaction
Main Characteristics: Battery-powered devices connect intermittently to save power, leading to status discontinuities.
Do's: Handle network outages gracefully; explain to users why devices might be out of sync.
Don'ts: Assume constant connectivity; show conflicting status information without explanation.
Severity: Medium - Impacts user trust and conceptual model of the system.

Principle: Flexibility Usability Trade-off
Main Characteristics: As the flexibility of a system increases, its usability often decreases.
Do's: Strike a balance between feature richness and ease of use; use secondary devices for complex rare tasks.
Don'ts: Bloat a simple device interface with capabilities that are rarely needed.
Severity: Medium - Critical for agent decision-making regarding feature prioritization.

Principle: Cognitive Dissonance in Purchasing
Main Characteristics: Users experience doubt, regret, or anxiety after a purchase (internal inconsistency).
Do's: Reassure customers after a sale; provide honest information pre-purchase.
Don'ts: Share insufficient information that leads to misaligned expectations.
Other Characteristics: Brand Story / Psychology of Conversion; E-commerce/SaaS.
Severity: Medium - Affects long-term user retention and brand relationship.

Principle: Habit Loop (Cue-Routine-Reward)
Main Characteristics: Habits are automatic behaviors triggered by a cue and reinforced by a reward.
Do's: Identify a clear, unambiguous cue; provide an immediate meaningful reward.
Don'ts: Expect users to repeat complex, varying tasks to form a habit; use ambiguous triggers.
Other Characteristics: How People Remember / Behavior Change; Health/Fitness apps.
Severity: Medium - Key to sustainable behavior change without constant willpower.

Principle: Principle 4: Business people and developers must work together daily
Main Characteristics: Reduces communication gaps and fosters sensitivity to business drivers within the production team.
Do's: Include designers in daily business strategy and product initiative conversations.
Don'ts: Allow development managers to be the only ones attending high-level business meetings.
Other Characteristics: How People Decide (UX lens); Strategic business context.

Principle: Principle 5: Build projects around motivated individuals
Main Characteristics: Provides an environment of support and trust to empower team decision-making.
Do's: Match work to individual skillsets and interests to yield better results and higher morale.
Don'ts: Treat staff as interchangeable resources without regard for strengths or skills.
Other Characteristics: How People Focus (UX lens); Team organizational context.

Principle: Principle 11: The best architectures, requirements, and designs emerge from self-organizing teams
Main Characteristics: Empowers the people closest to the project to control the timing and flow of their work.
Do's: Work collaboratively on design and allow developers with a 'good eye' to contribute.
Don'ts: Control every design decision from a central authority without team input.
Other Characteristics: How People Work (UX lens); Collaborative team structure.

Principle: People imagine objects tilted and at a slight angle above
Main Characteristics: The 'canonical perspective' is the universal way people remember and recognize objects.
Do's: Draw icons and objects from a perspective slightly above and offset to the side.
Don'ts: Show objects from a top-down or direct 2D perspective if fast recognition is needed.

Principle: People can miss changes in their visual fields
Main Characteristics: Inattention blindness occurs when users are focused on a task and don't expect changes.
Do's: Add visual cues like blinking or beeps to signal a refreshed screen change.
Don'ts: Assume a user noticed a change just because it appeared on the screen.

Principle: Red and blue together are hard on the eyes
Main Characteristics: Chromostereopsis causes depth perception issues where one color jumps out and another recedes.
Do's: Maintain high contrast between foreground and background.
Don'ts: Place blue and red or green and red near each other; use blue text on a red background.

Principle: Standardized paper sizes (DIN) facilitate storage and impact.
Main Characteristics: Standard sizes (A-series) fit into files, indexes, and standard envelopes efficiently.
Do's: Employ standardized DIN system sizes for printed matter.
Don'ts: Use custom sizes that result in paper waste and increased production costs unless absolutely necessary.

Principle: Marginal proportions influenced by the Golden Section enhance reading pleasure.
Main Characteristics: A well-proportioned margin provides technical safety for trimming and aesthetic relief.
Do's: Use mathematical formulas like the Golden Section to calculate margins; make margins wide enough to hold the book without covering text.
Don'ts: Make margins too narrow (risk of text mutilation during trimming) or exactly equal (creates dullness).

Principle: The fewer the differences in the size of the illustrations, the quieter the impression created by the design.
Main Characteristics: Uniformity in visual elements reduces visual noise and promotes a sense of calm and order.
Do's: Reduce pictorial elements to a few formats of the same size.
Don'ts: Use many different picture sizes unless intended to create a restless, high-energy effect.
Other Characteristics: How People See; Layout Design Principles

Principle: Program to an Interface, not an Implementation
Main Characteristics: Depend on abstractions rather than concrete classes to ensure flexibility for future extensions.
Do's: Determine what an object needs from another; describe methods in an interface/abstract class; make dependencies implement that interface.
Don'ts: Make classes tightly coupled to concrete employee or collaborator classes.
Severity: Medium (Improves flexibility and extensibility without breaking existing code)

Principle: Interface Segregation Principle (SOLID)
Main Characteristics: Clients shouldn't be forced to depend on methods they do not use.
Do's: Break down 'fat' interfaces into granular, specific ones; implement only necessary methods.
Don'ts: Cram tons of unrelated methods into a single interface; force clients to implement stubs for unused methods.
Severity: Medium (Reduces impact of interface changes on unrelated client code)

Principle: F-Pattern Alignment
Main Characteristics: Users typically scan digital content from top to bottom along the left edge.
Do's: Align buttons and Call-to-Action elements along the left edge or at the bottom of descriptive content.
Don'ts: Place the strongest visual element in the middle of a scan path, forcing eyes to travel back and forth.
Other Characteristics: UI Design; How People See; Desktop/Mobile context.
Severity: Medium (Optimizes for natural human scanning behaviors and reduces cognitive effort)

Principle: Direction over Choice
Main Characteristics: Reducing the number of options helps users focus on the primary task.
Do's: Limit formatting options in editors to encourage writing; use single-column layouts for focus.
Don'ts: Overwhelm users with infinite settings or buttons that distract from core purpose.
Other Characteristics: Psychological Category: How People Decide; Case: Medium Editor.

Principle: Visual Loudness Scaling
Main Characteristics: Elements should have a visual volume corresponding to their importance.
Do's: Use high-contrast typography for major headers; use 'Scream' modules for promotions and 'Whisper' for tips.
Don'ts: Make secondary information as loud as primary calls to action.
Other Characteristics: Psychological Category: How People Scan; Category: Functional Patterns.

Principle: Timelessness over Trend
Main Characteristics: Prioritizing long-term utility and accessibility over current stylistic trends.
Do's: Stick to proven UI patterns; prioritize performance over flashy effects (e.g., parallax).
Don'ts: Adopt new technologies or styles purely for novelty if they hinder the user experience.
Other Characteristics: Psychological Category: Mental Models; Case: TED.

Principle: Social Proof / Connection
Main Characteristics: Users are motivated by the presence and feedback of others.
Do's: Show discussion threads; display learner counts; use visible testimonials.
Don'ts: Hide social interactions in areas intended for collaborative learning.
Other Characteristics: Psychological Category: Social Learning; Case: FutureLearn.

Principle: People reconstruct memories every time they remember them
Main Characteristics: Memories are not static; they change over time and the most vivid ones are often erroneous.
Do's: Focus design on the memory that will remain rather than just the immediate experience; use 'start strong, end strong' strategies.
Don'ts: Don't assume user feedback after a task is a 100% accurate representation of the literal events.

Principle: Anecdotes are more persuasive than data
Main Characteristics: Personal stories have more power to influence human belief and memory than raw data points.
Do's: Use storytelling and user scenarios to communicate design value and persuade stakeholders.
Don'ts: Don't present data in isolation without a narrative context.

Principle: Surpassing expectations creates lasting memories
Main Characteristics: Surprising users with positive, unexpected value creates a much stronger mental impression than standard service.
Do's: Incorporate 'Easter eggs' or helpful unexpected alerts (e.g., Gmail's missing attachment reminder).
Don'ts: Not in source

Principle: Animation is a tool for brand messaging
Main Characteristics: Motion conveys personality, tone, and brand perspective, similar to color and typography.
Do's: Establish a consistent motion vocabulary that reflects brand attributes; use custom easing curves.
Don'ts: Use mismatched or arbitrary animations that create a 'mish-mash' experience; use default OS easing for branded elements.
Other Characteristics: How People Perceive; Platform: Design Systems
